name: Laptop Hardware Components
description: Internal components of laptops including batteries, keyboards, RAM, storage, and display types
key_facts: SO-DIMM is the small form factor RAM used in laptops
Laptop batteries are typically lithium-ion (Li-ion) and rated in watt-hours (Wh)
M.2 slots are used for SSDs and Wi-Fi cards in modern laptops
OLED and IPS are common laptop display panel technologies
Laptop keyboards use a scissor-switch or butterfly mechanism
common_misconceptions: Desktop RAM (DIMM) fits in laptops (laptops use smaller SO-DIMM modules)
All M.2 slots support NVMe (some only support SATA protocol)

name: Display Technologies
description: Types of displays and connectors used in mobile and desktop devices
key_facts: LCD types include TN (fastest response), IPS (best color/viewing angles), and VA (best contrast)
OLED displays produce their own light and do not need a backlight
Common display connectors: HDMI, DisplayPort, USB-C with DisplayPort Alt Mode, VGA (legacy)
Touchscreen technologies include capacitive (most common) and resistive
common_misconceptions: Higher resolution always means better display quality (panel type, brightness, and color accuracy also matter)
HDMI and DisplayPort are interchangeable (they use different protocols and pin configurations)

name: Network Cables and Connectors
description: Types of network cabling including copper and fiber optic, their categories, and connector types
key_facts: Cat 5e supports up to 1 Gbps at 100 meters
Cat 6 supports up to 10 Gbps at 55 meters and 1 Gbps at 100 meters
Cat 6a supports 10 Gbps at the full 100 meters
RJ-45 is the standard Ethernet connector; RJ-11 is used for telephone
Fiber optic types: single-mode (long distance, small core) and multimode (shorter distance, larger core)
common_misconceptions: Cat 6 supports 10 Gbps at 100 meters (it only supports 10 Gbps up to 55 meters)
All fiber optic cables are the same (single-mode and multimode have different core sizes and distance capabilities)

name: TCP/IP Fundamentals
description: TCP/IP model layers, IP addressing, subnetting basics, and common protocols
key_facts: IPv4 addresses are 32-bit, written as four octets in dotted decimal notation
IPv6 addresses are 128-bit, written in hexadecimal colon notation
Common ports: HTTP (80), HTTPS (443), DNS (53), DHCP (67/68), SSH (22), RDP (3389)
TCP is connection-oriented with a three-way handshake; UDP is connectionless
DHCP automatically assigns IP addresses; DNS resolves domain names to IP addresses
common_misconceptions: UDP is unreliable and should never be used (UDP is preferred for real-time applications like VoIP and streaming)
Every device needs a public IP address (NAT allows multiple devices to share one public IP)

name: Wireless Networking Standards
description: Wi-Fi standards, frequencies, and wireless configuration concepts
key_facts: Wi-Fi 5 (802.11ac) operates on 5 GHz only, up to 3.5 Gbps
Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) operates on 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz, up to 9.6 Gbps
Wi-Fi 6E (802.11ax) extends into the 6 GHz band for additional channels
2.4 GHz has longer range but fewer non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11)
5 GHz has more channels and less interference but shorter range
common_misconceptions: 5 GHz is always better than 2.4 GHz (2.4 GHz has better range and wall penetration)
Wi-Fi 6 requires new cabling (Wi-Fi standards affect wireless only, not Ethernet cabling)

name: Motherboards and CPUs
description: Motherboard form factors, chipsets, CPU sockets, and processor characteristics
key_facts: Common motherboard form factors: ATX (full size), Micro-ATX, Mini-ITX
Intel uses LGA (Land Grid Array) sockets; AMD uses PGA (Pin Grid Array) and AM5 LGA
CPU specifications include clock speed (GHz), core count, cache size, and TDP
Multithreading (Intel Hyper-Threading / AMD SMT) allows each core to handle two threads
The chipset determines supported features like PCIe lanes, USB ports, and SATA connections
common_misconceptions: Higher clock speed always means better performance (core count, cache, and architecture also matter)
Any CPU fits any motherboard (CPUs must match the specific socket type)

name: RAM and Storage Devices
description: Types of memory (RAM) and storage technologies including HDDs, SSDs, and RAID configurations
key_facts: DDR4 operates at 1.2V; DDR5 operates at 1.1V with higher speeds
DIMM modules are for desktops; SO-DIMM for laptops
NVMe SSDs connect via M.2 or PCIe and are faster than SATA SSDs
RAID 0 is striping (performance, no redundancy); RAID 1 is mirroring (redundancy)
RAID 5 requires minimum 3 drives, uses parity for single-drive fault tolerance
common_misconceptions: RAID is a backup solution (RAID provides redundancy but is not a substitute for backups)
More RAM always improves performance (beyond a point, other bottlenecks limit gains)

name: Power Supplies and Peripheral Devices
description: Power supply units, connectors, and common peripheral devices and interfaces
key_facts: PSU wattage must exceed total system power draw; use a PSU calculator for sizing
Common PSU connectors: 24-pin ATX motherboard, 8-pin CPU, 6/8-pin PCIe, SATA, Molex
80 Plus certification rates PSU efficiency: Bronze (82%), Gold (87%), Platinum (89%), Titanium (90%+)
USB standards: USB 2.0 (480 Mbps), USB 3.0 (5 Gbps), USB 3.1 Gen 2 (10 Gbps), USB 4 (40 Gbps)
USB-C is a connector type that can carry USB 3.x, Thunderbolt, DisplayPort, and power delivery
common_misconceptions: USB-C always means USB 3.x speeds (USB-C is just a connector shape and can carry USB 2.0)
A higher wattage PSU uses more electricity (PSUs only draw the power the system needs)

name: Virtualization Concepts
description: Hypervisor types, virtual machine management, and cloud computing service models
key_facts: Type 1 hypervisors run directly on hardware (VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen)
Type 2 hypervisors run on top of an OS (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation)
Cloud service models: IaaS (infrastructure), PaaS (platform), SaaS (software)
VMs require allocated CPU, RAM, storage, and network resources from the host
Snapshots capture the state of a VM at a point in time for quick rollback
common_misconceptions: Virtualization and cloud computing are the same (virtualization is a technology; cloud computing is a service delivery model)
VMs can use unlimited host resources (VMs are limited by allocated and available host resources)

name: Printer Technologies and Troubleshooting
description: Laser and inkjet printer operations, maintenance, and common issues
key_facts: Laser printing process: Processing, Charging, Exposing, Developing, Transferring, Fusing, Cleaning
Inkjet printers use piezoelectric or thermal technology to spray ink droplets
Common laser printer issues: ghosting (worn drum), faded prints (low toner), paper jams
Thermal printers use heat-sensitive paper and require no ink or toner
Print spooler manages the queue of print jobs sent to a printer
common_misconceptions: Laser printers use ink (they use toner, a dry powder fused by heat)
Clearing a paper jam only requires removing visible paper (residual paper fragments can remain inside)

name: Hardware and Network Troubleshooting Methodology
description: Systematic approach to diagnosing and resolving hardware, network, and peripheral issues
key_facts: CompTIA troubleshooting steps: Identify the problem, Establish a theory, Test the theory, Establish a plan, Implement the solution, Verify functionality, Document findings
POST (Power-On Self-Test) beep codes indicate hardware failures during startup
Blue Screen of Death (BSOD) typically indicates driver, hardware, or memory issues
Use Device Manager to check for driver issues indicated by yellow exclamation marks
Network troubleshooting tools: ping, tracert/traceroute, ipconfig/ifconfig, nslookup
common_misconceptions: Rebooting always fixes the problem (it may mask underlying issues that need proper diagnosis)
All BSODs are caused by hardware failure (software drivers and OS corruption are common causes)
